text:
Motivation


Evaluate dynamic predictions of imminent delivery (7 days) in pregnancies complicated by early-onset fetal growth restriction.
Main goal \(\longrightarrow\) optimizing antenatal corticosteroid (CCS) administration







OPTICORE data \(\longrightarrow\) multicenter cohort study in the Netherlands

Asymmetric misclassification




TP


FP


TN


FN

Definitions and notation


\(i=1,\dotsc,n\) individuals
\(\require{color}\textcolor{#ba1732}{T_i^*}\) true time until event
\(\require{color}\textcolor{#ba1732}{C_i}\) right-censoring times
\(\require{color}\textcolor{#ba1732}{T_i=\min(T_i^*, C_i)}\) observed times
\(\require{color}\textcolor{#ba1732}{\delta_i=\mathbf{1}\{T_i^*\leq C_i\}}\) censoring indicator
\(\require{color}\textcolor{#ba1732}{L_i}\) left-truncation times
\(\require{color}\textcolor{#ba1732}{\boldsymbol{\mathcal{H}}_i(t)}\) available history at time \(t\)




Dynamic predictions



\[
  \normalsize\require{color}
  \pi_{i}(s \mid t)=\operatorname{Pr}\left(T_{i}^* \leq t+s \mid T_{i}^*>t, \colorbox{#ba1732}{$\color{#ffffff}\boldsymbol{\mathcal{H}}_i(t)$}\right)
  \]

Scoring rules











A scoring rule is proper if

\[ E\left[\mathcal{S}\big(\pi_i^{\text{true}}(s\mid t), D_i(t,s)\big) \right] \leq E\left[\mathcal{S}\big(\widehat{\pi}_i(s\mid t), D_i(t,s)\big) \right]. \]

It is strictly proper if it holds with equality if and only if \(\pi_i^{\text{true}}(s\mid t)\equiv \widehat{\pi}_i(s\mid t)\).





It is centered if \(\mathcal{S}(1,1)=\mathcal{S}(0,0)=0\).

Scoring rules: limitations




They quantify predictive accuracy, but not the consequences of decisions based on predictions.





They do not account for the asymmetric harms of false-positives and false-negatives decisions.

Clinical utility





Focus on the quality of decisions driven by dynamic predictions.





Based on a clinically relevant decision threshold \(c\in(0,1)\).







Widely used clinical utility metric \(\longrightarrow\) net benefit (NB)
Expected cost (EC):

\[\normalsize\require{color}\begin{split}
        \begin{split}
        \text{EC}_c\big(\pi_i(s\mid t),\, & D_i(t,s)\big) =  c\mathbf{1}\{\pi_i(s\mid t)\geq c\} (1-D_i(t,s))\\
        &+(1-c)\mathbf{1}\{\pi_i(s\mid t)< c\}D_i(t,s)
    \end{split}
    \end{split} \]






NB and EC rely on a single decision threshold \(c\in (0,1)\)

Integrated weighted expected cost


To avoid reliance on a single \(c\) and achieve strict properness:

\[\normalsize\require{color}\begin{split} & \text{IWEC}_w \big(\pi_i(s\mid t), D_i(t,s)\big) = \\ & \int_0^1\text{EC}_c\big(\pi_i(s\mid t),D_i(t,s)\big)w(c)dc \end{split} \]

\(w(\cdot)\) \(\longrightarrow\) positive weight function reflecting the clinical relevance of different \(c\)’s




To avoid reliance on a single \(c\) and achieve strict properness:

\[\normalsize\require{color}\begin{split} & \text{IWEC}_w \big(\pi_i(s\mid t), D_i(t,s)\big) = \\ & \textcolor{#ba1732}{\int_0^1}\text{EC}_c\big(\pi_i(s\mid t),D_i(t,s)\big)\textcolor{#ba1732}{w(c)dc} \end{split} \]

\(w(\cdot)\) \(\longrightarrow\) positive weight function reflecting the clinical relevance of different \(c\)’s




To avoid reliance on a single \(c\) and achieve strict properness:

\[\normalsize\require{color}\begin{split} & \text{IWEC}_w \big(\pi_i(s\mid t), D_i(t,s)\big) = \\ & \textcolor{#ba1732}{\int_0^1}\text{EC}_c\big(\pi_i(s\mid t),D_i(t,s)\big)\textcolor{#ba1732}{w(c)dc} \end{split} \]

In practice \(\longrightarrow\) beta family of weight functions \(w(c;\alpha,\beta)\)







Weight function \(\longrightarrow\) all thresholds \(c\in (0,1)\) and its relative clinical importance.






















A scoring rule is centered and strictly proper \(\Longleftrightarrow\) it can be represented by \(\text{IWEC}_w \big(\pi_i(s\mid t), D_i(t,s)\big)\) for some \(w(\cdot)\).

\(w(c)=1\) \(\longrightarrow\) Brier score
\(w(c)=c^{-1}(1-c)^{-1}\) \(\longrightarrow\) logarithmic score

Estimation under LTRC data

To estimate:

\[\text{eIWEC}_w(t,s)=E\big[\text{IWEC}_w\big(\pi(s\mid t),D(t,s)\big) \mid T^*>t\big]\]

We propose two approaches:

A nonparametric IPW estimator.
A model-based estimator.

We established consistency and asymptotic normality.

Simulation study


We evaluate dynamic predictions within \((1,3]\) obtained via three Cox proportional-hazards models:

The true model \(\longrightarrow\) \(M_\text{True}\)
Two misspecified models \(\longrightarrow\) \(M_1, M_2\)




To reflect the asymmetry between FP and FN in our clinical context, we set \(c\in(0.1, 0.3)\).

Application: OPTICORE


Goal \(\longrightarrow\) outperform current clinical practice for CCS administration.
In the Netherlands:

Strategy 1 \(\longrightarrow\) when pulsatility index of the umbilical artery becomes abnormal
Strategy 2 \(\longrightarrow\) when an absent or reversed end-diastolic velocity of the umbilical artery is detected





Joint model considering four different fetal outcomes:

\[\scriptsize\begin{split}
\textcolor{#BA1732}{\text{JM}}:  h_{i}\left(t \mid \boldsymbol{\mathcal{H}}_{i}(t), \boldsymbol{X}_{i}\right) =h_{0}(t) \exp \left(  \gamma\boldsymbol{X}_{i} + \sum_{j=1}^4\alpha_j f_j\{Y_{ji}(t)\} \right)
\end{split}\]

Dynamic predictions \(\longrightarrow\) \((182, 189]\) days = \((26, 27]\) weeks of gestation.
We consider \(c\in(0.1,0.3)\) and \(w(c;5.16, 17.62)\).

Contribution


Extension of clinical utility to dynamic prediction.
IWEC and weighted decision curves.
A comprehensive estimation framework for LTRC data.
Methods will be available in the JMbayes2 R package.

Limitations & future research

Limitations \(\longrightarrow\) Reducing the time-to-event outcome to a binary outcome discards relevant information.





Future work \(\longrightarrow\) Identifying the best-performing joint model and translating it into an online tool for obstetricians to generate dynamic risk predictions for delivery.

Challenges of the data

CCS cannot be administered until the fetus reaches \(500\) grams and a gestational age of \(168\) days (\(24\) weeks).

Challenges of the data


Under LTRC data \(D_i(t,s)=\mathbf{1}\{t<T_i^*\leq t+s\}\) is unobserved for \(i\) censored within \((t, t+s]\) or \(L_i>t\).





Under LTRC data \(D_i(t,s)=\mathbf{1}\{t<T_i^*\leq t+s\}\) is unobserved for \(i\) censored within \((t, t+s]\) or \(L_i>t\).

IPW estimator


We define the IPW estimator:

\[\scriptsize\require{color} \widehat{\text{eIWEC}}^{\text{IPW}}_w(t,s)=\frac{1}{n_t} \sum_{i:L_i<t<T_i} \widehat{W}_i(t,s)\text{IWEC}_w\big(\pi_i(s\mid t),\tilde{D}_i(t,s)\big)\]

\(n_t\) \(\longrightarrow\) number of individuals at risk at \(t\)
\(\tilde{D}_i(t,s)\) \(\longrightarrow\) the observed binary indicator
\(\widehat{W}_i(t,s)\) \(\longrightarrow\) adjust for LTRC data via left-truncated Kaplan–Meier of the censoring distribution




We define the IPW estimator:

\[ \scriptsize\require{color}\widehat{\text{eIWEC}}^{\text{IPW}}_w(t,s)=\frac{1}{\textcolor{#BA1732}{n_t}} \sum_{i:L_i<t<T_i} \widehat{W}_i(t,s)\text{IWEC}_w\big(\pi_i(s\mid t),\tilde{D}_i(t,s)\big)\]

\(\textcolor{#BA1732}{n_t}\) \(\longrightarrow\) number of individuals at risk at \(t\)
\(\tilde{D}_i(t,s)\) \(\longrightarrow\) the observed binary indicator
\(\widehat{W}_i(t,s)\) \(\longrightarrow\) adjust for LTRC data via left-truncated Kaplan–Meier of the censoring distribution




We define the IPW estimator:

\[\scriptsize\require{color} \widehat{\text{eIWEC}}^{\text{IPW}}_w(t,s)=\frac{1}{n_t} \sum_{i:L_i<t<T_i} \widehat{W}_i(t,s)\text{IWEC}_w\big(\pi_i(s\mid t),\textcolor{#BA1732}{\tilde{D}_i(t,s)}\big)\]

\(n_t\) \(\longrightarrow\) number of individuals at risk at \(t\)
\(\textcolor{#BA1732}{\tilde{D}_i(t,s)}\) \(\longrightarrow\) the observed binary indicator
\(\widehat{W}_i(t,s)\) \(\longrightarrow\) adjust for LTRC data via left-truncated Kaplan–Meier of the censoring distribution




We define the IPW estimator:

\[\scriptsize\require{color} \widehat{\text{eIWEC}}^{\text{IPW}}_w(t,s)=\frac{1}{n_t} \sum_{i:L_i<t<T_i} \textcolor{#BA1732}{\widehat{W}_i(t,s)}\text{IWEC}_w\big(\pi_i(s\mid t),\tilde{D}_i(t,s)\big)\]

\(n_t\) \(\longrightarrow\) number of individuals at risk at \(t\)
\(\tilde{D}_i(t,s)\) \(\longrightarrow\) the observed binary indicator
\(\textcolor{#BA1732}{\widehat{W}_i(t,s)}\) \(\longrightarrow\) adjust for LTRC data via left-truncated Kaplan–Meier of the censoring distribution

Model-based estimator


We define the model-based estimator:

\[\scriptsize\require{color}\begin{split}
        \widehat{\text{eIWEC}}^{\text{MB}}_w(t,s)=  \frac{1}{n_t}&\sum_{i=1}^n  \delta_i\mathbf{1}\{L_i<t<T_i\leq t+s\}\text{IWEC}_w\{\pi_i(s\mid t),1\} \\
        &+ \mathbf{1}\{T_i>t+s\}\mathbf{1}\{L_i<t\}\text{IWEC}_w\{\pi_i(s\mid t),0\} \\
        & + (1-\delta_i)\mathbf{1}\{L_i<t<T_i\leq t+s\}\left[\pi_i(s\mid t)\text{IWEC}_w\{\pi_i(s\mid t),1\} \\
        + (1-\pi_i(s\mid t))\text{IWEC}_w\{\pi_i(s\mid t),0\} \right].
    \end{split}\]



We define the model-based estimator:

\[\scriptsize\require{color}\begin{split}
        \widehat{\text{eIWEC}}^{\text{MB}}_w(t,s)=  \frac{1}{n_t}&\sum_{i=1}^n  \textcolor{#BA1732}{\delta_i\mathbf{1}\{L_i<t<T_i\leq t+s\}}\text{IWEC}_w\{\pi_i(s\mid t),\textcolor{#BA1732}{1}\} \\
        &+ \mathbf{1}\{T_i>t+s\}\mathbf{1}\{L_i<t\}\text{IWEC}_w\{\pi_i(s\mid t),0\} \\
        & + (1-\delta_i)\mathbf{1}\{L_i<t<T_i\leq t+s\}\left[\pi_i(s\mid t)\text{IWEC}_w\{\pi_i(s\mid t),1\} \\
        + (1-\pi_i(s\mid t))\text{IWEC}_w\{\pi_i(s\mid t),0\} \right].
    \end{split}\]



We define the model-based estimator:

\[\scriptsize\require{color}\begin{split}
        \widehat{\text{eIWEC}}^{\text{MB}}_w(t,s)=  \frac{1}{n_t}&\sum_{i=1}^n  \delta_i\mathbf{1}\{L_i<t<T_i\leq t+s\}\text{IWEC}_w\{\pi_i(s\mid t),1\} \\
        &+ \textcolor{#BA1732}{\mathbf{1}\{T_i>t+s\}\mathbf{1}\{L_i<t\}}\text{IWEC}_w\{\pi_i(s\mid t),\textcolor{#BA1732}{0}\} \\
        & + (1-\delta_i)\mathbf{1}\{L_i<t<T_i\leq t+s\}\left[\pi_i(s\mid t)\text{IWEC}_w\{\pi_i(s\mid t),1\} \\
        + (1-\pi_i(s\mid t))\text{IWEC}_w\{\pi_i(s\mid t),0\} \right].
    \end{split}\]



We define the model-based estimator:

\[\scriptsize\require{color}\begin{split}
        \widehat{\text{eIWEC}}^{\text{MB}}_w(t,s)=  \frac{1}{n_t}&\sum_{i=1}^n  \delta_i\mathbf{1}\{L_i<t<T_i\leq t+s\}\text{IWEC}_w\{\pi_i(s\mid t),1\} \\
        &+ \mathbf{1}\{T_i>t+s\}\mathbf{1}\{L_i<t\}\text{IWEC}_w\{\pi_i(s\mid t),0\} \\
        & + \textcolor{#BA1732}{(1-\delta_i)\mathbf{1}\{L_i<t<T_i\leq t+s\}}\left[\textcolor{#BA1732}{\pi_i(s\mid t)}\text{IWEC}_w\{\pi_i(s\mid t),\textcolor{#BA1732}{1}\} \\
        + \textcolor{#BA1732}{(1-\pi_i(s\mid t))}\text{IWEC}_w\{\pi_i(s\mid t),\textcolor{#BA1732}{0}\} \right].
    \end{split}\]

Large sample properties


We demonstrated that both the IPW and model-based estimators are:

Consistent
Asymptotically normal





This result applies to all centered strictly proper metrics:

Brier score
Logarithmic score

The joint model


Linear mixed-effects submodels:

\[ \require{color} Y_{ji}(t) = m_{ji}(t) + \varepsilon_{ji}(t), \quad j=1,\dots, 4 \]

\(m_{ji}(t)\) \(\longrightarrow\) natural cubic splines
\(\varepsilon_{ji}(t)\sim N(0,\sigma^2)\)




Linear mixed-effects submodels:

\[ \require{color} Y_{ji}(t) = \colorbox{#BA1732}{$\color{#ffffff}m_{1i}(t)$} + \varepsilon_{1i}(t), \quad j=1,\dots, 4 \]
